section.title: Love as Strong as Death
section.sectionRationale: This is the great climax and key of the whole Song. As the couple comes up from the wilderness, the bride speaks the poem's central truth: set me as a seal over your heart, for love is as strong as death, its flame the fiercest of all, and no waters can quench it and no wealth can buy it. The closing verses gather up the bride's integrity, the worthlessness of love that is bought, and the final call, come away, my beloved.
section.thesis: Love is as strong as death and cannot be quenched or bought; the bride asks to be sealed on her beloved's heart, and the Song closes with her integrity honored and the final invitation, come away, my beloved.
section.frameNote: Three movements: coming up from the wilderness, and the Song's crowning word, set me as a seal, for love is as strong as death and unquenchable and unpurchasable; the bride's integrity as a wall and the worthlessness of Solomon's bought vineyard beside her own freely given love; and the closing exchange, let me hear your voice, and, come away, my beloved.

verses.text: Who is this coming up from the wilderness, leaning on her beloved? I roused you under the apple tree; there your mother conceived you; there she travailed and brought you forth.

Set me as a {{chotam|seal}} over your heart, as a seal upon your arm. For {{ahavah|love}} is as strong as death, its {{qinah|jealousy}} as unrelenting as Sheol. Its sparks are fiery flames, the fiercest blaze of all.
Mighty waters cannot quench love; rivers cannot sweep it away. If a man were to give all the wealth of his house for love, his offer would be utterly scorned.

units.label: The bride's integrity and the unbought vineyard
units.note: a wall, her own to give

verses.text: We have a little sister, and her breasts are not yet grown. What shall we do for our sister on the day she is spoken for?
If she is a wall, we will build a tower of silver upon her. If she is a door, we will enclose her with panels of cedar.
I am a wall, and my breasts are like towers. So I have become in his eyes like one who brings peace.
Solomon had a vineyard in Baal-hamon. He leased it to the tenants. For its fruit, each was to bring a thousand shekels of silver.
But my own vineyard is mine to give; the thousand shekels are for you, O Solomon, and two hundred are for those who tend its fruit.
You who dwell in the gardens, my companions are listening for your voice. Let me hear it!
Come away, my beloved, and be like a gazelle or a young stag on the mountains of spices.

chotam.gloss: Seal, signet. A seal marked ownership and was worn close, over the heart or on the arm. The bride asks to be set as a seal on her beloved, love as belonging, permanence, and pledge, held always near.

ahavah.gloss: Love. The word that crowns the whole Song here: love is as strong as death, unquenchable by any flood, unpurchasable by any wealth. This is the covenant love the Song has celebrated, now named in its full strength.

qinah.gloss: Jealousy, ardent zeal. Not petty envy but the fierce, exclusive devotion that will not share the beloved; as unrelenting as the grave, the same zeal Scripture ascribes to God for His people.

basic.context: This is the summit of the whole Song, its key verse and crowning truth. As the couple comes up from the wilderness, leaning together, the bride speaks the words the whole book has been moving toward: set me as a seal over your heart, for love is as strong as death. Its flame is the fiercest of all; no flood can quench it and no fortune can buy it. Then the closing scene honors the bride's integrity, she is a wall, her love her own to give, not for sale like Solomon's leased vineyard, and the Song ends as it lived, with the call, come away, my beloved.
basic.hermeneutics: On the plain level this is the Song's great statement about covenant love itself: that true love is powerful, permanent, exclusive, and beyond price. It cannot be extinguished or purchased, only given. Within the whole Bible, this fierce, unquenchable, jealous love is supremely the love of God for His people, love as strong as death that met death itself at the cross, and a flame that many waters could not quench.
basic.originalLanguages: The bride asks to be a chotam, a seal, worn over the heart, love as belonging and pledge. Then comes the crowning word, ahavah, love, as strong as death, and qinah, jealousy, the fierce, exclusive zeal that will not share the beloved, as unrelenting as the grave. Its flame is the fiercest of all, unquenchable and beyond all price.
basic.typology: Love as strong as death, meeting death and not being overcome, foreshadows the love of Christ that went into death and rose again. The seal over the heart pictures the pledge of belonging, and the Spirit given as the seal of those who are Christ's. The unpurchasable love points to a salvation that cannot be bought but only received.
basic.christ: Love is as strong as death, the Song says, and at the cross that love met death itself and proved stronger, for Christ loved His people to the end and death could not hold Him. The jealous, unquenchable flame is His love that no flood of judgment could put out. And a man cannot buy this love with all his wealth; it is given, as Christ gave Himself, and received by faith, not purchased.
basic.god: God's love for His people is fierce, faithful, and jealous in the holiest sense: He will not share them with idols and will not let them go. It is as strong as death and stronger, unquenchable and beyond price. He sets His people as a seal on His heart and seals them with His Spirit as the pledge that they are His.
basic.crossReferences: Romans 8:38 to 39, nothing can separate us from the love of God. 1 Corinthians 15:54 to 55, death swallowed up in victory. Ephesians 1:13 to 14, sealed with the Holy Spirit as a pledge. Exodus 34:14, the LORD whose name is Jealous.
basic.oneStory: The Song reaches its summit: love is as strong as death, unquenchable and beyond price, a seal worn over the heart. It names the covenant love the whole book has celebrated and points to its source, the love of God in Christ, which met death and overcame it, and which no waters can quench and no wealth can buy.

context.literary: The climax and interpretive key of the Song, a compact poem on the nature of love, framed by the coming up from the wilderness and the closing call to the beloved. Verses 6 to 7 are the thematic center of the whole book; verses 8 to 12 return to the bride's integrity and freely given love, and verses 13 to 14 close the Song on the note of longing with which it began.
context.historical: A seal, or signet, marked ownership and identity and was worn over the heart or on the arm. Sheol is the grave, the realm of the dead. Solomon's vineyard leased for a thousand shekels evokes vast wealth, set against the bride's own vineyard, her love, which is hers to give and not for sale. The wall and door picture a woman's chastity and integrity.
context.canonical: The fierce, unquenchable, jealous love named here is the love Scripture ascribes above all to God, the LORD whose name is Jealous, whose love is stronger than death and from which nothing can separate His people, consummated in the marriage of the Lamb.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To crown the Song with its central truth, that love is powerful, permanent, exclusive, and beyond price, sealed on the heart of the beloved, and to close the poem honoring love freely given rather than bought.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: Verses 6 to 7 are less description than the Song's great affirmation about the nature of true love; the surrounding scenes describe the bride's integrity and the closing call, together commending love that is faithful, free, and not for sale.

debates.question: What kind of book is the Song: a celebration of married love, an allegory of Christ and the church, or both?

views.label: A celebration of covenant married love
views.case: The climax plainly praises the strength and worth of covenant love between husband and wife, faithful, unquenchable, and beyond price; Scripture honors such love as God's good gift, Genesis 2:24 and Proverbs 5:15 to 19.

views.label: An allegory of Christ and the church, or God and Israel
views.case: The church has long heard here the love of God, jealous and unquenchable, as strong as death, which met death at the cross and from which nothing can separate His people, in keeping with Exodus 34:14 and Romans 8:38 to 39.

views.label: Both, the literal love as a God-given sign
views.case: The wisest reading holds both: the poem truly crowns covenant married love, and because such love is a God-given sign of Christ and the church, Ephesians 5:31 to 32, love as strong as death rightly points to the love of God in Christ, its true source and fullest measure.

debates.bearing: Holding both keeps the passage whole: it honors the strength and worth of faithful marital love and finds its deepest ground in the love of God, which is stronger than death and cannot be bought.

study.originalLanguages: The bride asks to be a chotam, a seal, worn over the heart and on the arm, the mark of ownership and pledge. Then the Song speaks its crowning word: ahavah, love, is as strong as death; its qinah, jealousy, that fierce and exclusive zeal, is as unrelenting as Sheol, the grave. This is not petty envy but holy, faithful ardor, the same word Scripture uses of God's jealousy for His people. Love's flame is the fiercest of all, a blaze that many waters cannot quench and rivers cannot sweep away, and that no wealth can buy, for love is given, never purchased.

typology.type: Love as strong as death, unquenchable by many waters
typology.fulfillment: The love of Christ that entered death and overcame it, from which nothing can separate His people, Romans 8:38 to 39 and 1 Corinthians 15:54 to 55.

typology.type: The seal set over the heart
typology.fulfillment: The pledge of belonging, believers sealed with the Holy Spirit as the guarantee that they are Christ's, Ephesians 1:13 to 14.

typology.type: Love that no wealth can buy, only give
typology.fulfillment: A salvation that cannot be purchased but is freely given in Christ and received by faith, Isaiah 55:1 and Romans 6:23.

study.christ: Love is as strong as death: nowhere is this truer than at the cross, where the love of Christ went down into death itself and rose again unquenched. Many waters, the full flood of judgment, could not put out that flame, and death could not hold Him. His is a jealous love in the holiest sense: He will not share His people with idols and will not let them go, so that nothing in all creation can separate them from the love of God in Christ Jesus. And this love cannot be bought with all the wealth in the world; it is given, as He gave Himself, and received by faith. The bride's plea, set me as a seal on your heart, is answered as He seals His own with the Spirit, the pledge that they are forever His.
study.god: The love named here is above all God's love for His people: fierce, faithful, and jealous in the purest sense, for the LORD whose name is Jealous will not share His own with idols and will not lose them. His love is as strong as death and proves stronger; it cannot be quenched by any flood of judgment or bought at any price. He sets His people as a seal upon His heart, and He seals them with His Spirit as the guarantee that they belong to Him forever.

crossReferences.note: Neither death nor life nor anything else can separate us from the love of God in Christ Jesus, love as strong as death and stronger.
Death is swallowed up in victory; the love that is as strong as death meets and overcomes it in Christ.
Believers are sealed with the promised Holy Spirit, the pledge of their inheritance, answering set me as a seal.
The LORD, whose name is Jealous, is a jealous God, the holy zeal the Song names as love's jealousy.

study.oneStory: The Song reaches its summit in the truth it has been singing all along: love is as strong as death, a jealous, unquenchable flame that no flood can drown and no fortune can buy, sealed over the heart of the beloved. It crowns covenant love and traces it to its source, the love of God in Christ, which met death and overcame it, holds His people as His own, and can never be bought, only received. The Song ends where love always turns, come away, my beloved.

quiz.q: What does the bride ask of her beloved in 8:6?

options.text: Set me as a seal over your heart, as a seal upon your arm.
options.note: A seal marked ownership and pledge, worn close; she asks to be held as his own.

options.text: Set me free from any claim you have on me.
options.note: A seal is indeed a claim of belonging, but she asks him to press that claim more deeply, to hold her as his own, not to set her free from it.

options.text: Keep me at a safe distance.
options.note: The seal is worn close, over the heart, the opposite of distance.

options.text: Remember me only now and then.
options.note: A seal does keep her constantly before him, but as a permanent pledge worn close, not as an occasional thought now and then.

quiz.q: How does the Song describe the strength of love in 8:6-7?

options.text: As strong as death, an unquenchable flame that no waters can drown and no wealth can buy.
options.note: The Song's crowning statement about the power and worth of love.

options.text: As fragile as a passing mood.
options.note: Love does stir deep feeling, but the point is its strength and permanence, an unquenchable flame, not a fragile passing mood.

options.text: As something easily bought with enough money.
options.note: The passage does weigh love against wealth, but only to say that riches offered for love would be utterly scorned; love cannot be bought at any price.

options.text: As weaker than the smallest trouble.
options.note: Love here is as strong as death, not weak.

quiz.q: What does the bride mean when she says, I am a wall, in 8:10?

options.text: She has kept her integrity, and her love is her own to give.
options.note: The wall pictures chastity and integrity, contrasted with a door easily entered.

options.text: She is cold and closed off to all love.
options.note: The wall does speak of being guarded, but not cold or closed to all love; she has kept her integrity and freely gives her love to her beloved.

options.text: She has been bought like a leased vineyard.
options.note: The passage does set her beside Solomon's leased vineyard, but she is the opposite of it: her vineyard is her own to give, not bought or for sale.

options.text: She has nothing worth guarding.
options.note: She guards her integrity as something precious, a wall with towers.

quiz.q: Why can love not be bought, according to 8:7?

options.text: Because true love is given, not purchased; wealth offered for it would be scorned.
options.note: Love's worth is beyond price; it can only be freely given and received.

options.text: Because love is cheap and not worth much.
options.note: The opposite: love is beyond all price, so wealth cannot buy it.

options.text: Because the rich are simply too stingy.
options.note: Wealth is truly in view, but the point is the nature of love itself, priceless and freely given, not the stinginess of the wealthy.

options.text: Because only Solomon could afford it.
options.note: Solomon's great wealth is indeed set against love, but precisely to show that love is priceless and not for sale at any price, not that only the rich could buy it.

quiz.q: How does love as strong as death point to the gospel?

options.text: Christ's love went into death and overcame it, unquenchable and freely given, sealing His people as His own.
options.note: At the cross love met death and proved stronger; believers are sealed with the Spirit.

options.text: It teaches that we must earn God's love by our strength.
options.note: Love here is indeed strong, but it cannot be bought or earned by our strength; it is freely given, as Christ gave Himself.

options.text: It shows that death finally defeats love.
options.note: Love is as strong as death and stronger; in Christ death is swallowed up in victory.

options.text: It has no connection to Christ and the church.
options.note: It truly celebrates human love as strong as death, yet the church has long heard here the jealous, unquenchable love of God in Christ, Romans 8:38 to 39, so it does point to the gospel.

questions: Love is as strong as death. How does it steady you to know that the love of Christ went into death itself and came out stronger, so that nothing can separate you from it?
The bride asks to be a seal over her beloved's heart. Have you rested in the truth that Christ holds His people as His own and has sealed you with His Spirit?
Many waters cannot quench love. What floods, of trouble, guilt, or doubt, have felt able to put out God's love for you, and how does this verse answer them?
Love cannot be bought, only given. Where are you tempted to try to earn love, from God or others, that can only be received as a gift?
The bride kept her integrity, her love her own to give. What would it look like to guard and then freely give your love and devotion with that kind of wholeness?

translationNotes.note: A seal, chotam, marked ownership and was worn over the heart or on the arm; jealousy here, with a footnote passion, is fierce, exclusive devotion, not petty envy, the same zeal Scripture ascribes to God.
The fiercest blaze of all renders a Hebrew phrase that many take as a flame of the LORD, hinting that this love's ultimate source is God Himself.
A thousand shekels of silver, literally a thousand of silver, evokes great wealth; Solomon's leased vineyard is set against the bride's own vineyard, her love, which is hers to give and not for sale.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Song of Solomon 8:5-14, chapter 8. Verse text assembled by concatenating segment text values in order; the embedded speaker label The Bride within verse 5 was treated as a heading and excluded, and footnote markers were ignored. No em or en dashes occur in this section; none remain.
